med_suggestions.dosing: 875/125 mg PO BID x 5 days (prophylaxis) OR 7–10 days if established infection
med_suggestions.sig: 875/125 mg PO BID x 5 days (prophylaxis) OR 7–10 days if established infection
med_suggestions.notes: First-line for cat bites (high Pasteurella risk). Consider in-person eval for deep puncture, hand bites, immunocompromise, spreading cellulitis, or systemic symptoms.
med_suggestions.exclusions: Immediate-type beta-lactam allergy; significant renal impairment requires adjustment; severe hepatic disease with prior cholestatic jaundice from amox-clav

med_suggestions.dosing: Doxycycline 100 mg PO BID + Metronidazole 500 mg PO TID x 5–7 days (or 7–10 days if infection)
med_suggestions.sig: Doxycycline 100 mg PO BID + Metronidazole 500 mg PO TID x 5–7 days (or 7–10 days if infection)
med_suggestions.notes: Alternative for severe beta-lactam allergy when polymicrobial coverage needed.
med_suggestions.exclusions: Pregnancy; children <8 years (doxycycline); alcohol use with metronidazole; significant liver disease; drug interactions

med_suggestions.dosing: Clindamycin 300–450 mg PO TID + TMP-SMX DS (160/800) PO BID x 5–7 days (or 7–10 days if infection)
med_suggestions.sig: Clindamycin 300–450 mg PO TID + TMP-SMX DS (160/800) PO BID x 5–7 days (or 7–10 days if infection)
med_suggestions.notes: Alternative when MRSA risk is present or for beta-lactam allergy (clinician judgment).
med_suggestions.exclusions: Sulfa allergy; pregnancy considerations (avoid 1st trimester if possible and near term); C. difficile history/high CDI risk (clindamycin); infant jaundice/prematurity caution if breastfeeding with TMP-SMX

note.soap:
S:
34-year-old adult presents with a cat bite to the hand. No deep puncture or crush injury reported. No severe or spreading symptoms. No fever or chills. No immunocompromise. Tetanus status unknown. Patient is not pregnant.

O:
Phone history only; no physical exam or vital signs available.

A:
 - Working diagnosis (most likely): Cat bite wound to the hand without signs of severe infection.
 - Differential:
  1. Localized bacterial infection (early cellulitis)
  2. Pasteurella multocida infection
  3. Tetanus risk due to unknown immunization status
  4. Secondary soft tissue infection (abscess formation)
 - Red flags considered: No fever, no spreading redness or swelling, no deep puncture or crush injury, no immunocompromise.

P:
 - Disposition: Patient can be managed outpatient with close follow-up.
 - Plan: Amoxicillin-clavulanate — 875/125 mg PO BID x 5 days (prophylaxis) OR 7–10 days if established infection. Alternative: Doxycycline + Metronidazole (Doxycycline 100 mg PO BID + Metronidazole 500 mg PO TID x 5–7 days (or 7–10 days if infection)).
   Medication protocol (from data/protocols/meds):
   - Medication recommendations (Tier 1):
   - Reviewing physician will confirm appropriateness and prescribe if indicated.
   - Amoxicillin-clavulanate — 875/125 mg PO BID x 5 days (prophylaxis) OR 7–10 days if established infection
     - Notes: First-line for cat bites (high Pasteurella risk). Consider in-person eval for deep puncture, hand bites, immunocompromise, spreading cellulitis, or systemic symptoms.
     - Exclusions: Immediate-type beta-lactam allergy; significant renal impairment requires adjustment; severe hepatic disease with prior cholestatic jaundice from amox-clav
   - Doxycycline + Metronidazole — Doxycycline 100 mg PO BID + Metronidazole 500 mg PO TID x 5–7 days (or 7–10 days if infection)
     - Notes: Alternative for severe beta-lactam allergy when polymicrobial coverage needed.
     - Exclusions: Pregnancy; children <8 years (doxycycline); alcohol use with metronidazole; significant liver disease; drug interactions
   - Clindamycin + Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX) — Clindamycin 300–450 mg PO TID + TMP-SMX DS (160/800) PO BID x 5–7 days (or 7–10 days if infection)
     - Notes: Alternative when MRSA risk is present or for beta-lactam allergy (clinician judgment).
     - Exclusions: Sulfa allergy; pregnancy considerations (avoid 1st trimester if possible and near term); C. difficile history/high CDI risk (clindamycin); infant jaundice/prematurity caution if breastfeeding with TMP-SMX
   - Supportive care:
   - Local wound care: Copious irrigation if possible; gentle cleansing; keep clean/dry; avoid occlusive dressings unless directed.
   - Pain control: Acetaminophen (Tylenol) if safe. Ibuprofen (Advil) if safe and no contraindications.
   - Tetanus / rabies considerations: Assess tetanus status; rabies risk assessment based on animal status/local guidance; in-person evaluation if uncertain.
   - Follow-up and safety:
   - In-person evaluation recommended: Hand bites, deep puncture wounds, immunocompromise, rapidly spreading redness, severe pain, fever/chills, numbness/limited motion, or concern for tendon/joint involvement.
   - Urgent/ED now: Rapidly progressive swelling/redness, severe pain, fever/toxicity, red streaking, loss of function, or signs of systemic infection.
   - Initiate empiric antibiotic therapy appropriate for cat bite wounds (e.g., amoxicillin-clavulanate).
   - Review tetanus immunization status and update tetanus vaccine if indicated.
   - Educate patient on wound care, signs of infection, and when to seek urgent care.
 - Safety net: Advise patient to seek immediate care if increasing redness, swelling, pain, fever, or systemic symptoms develop.
patient_instructions: Cat bites have a high risk of infection. Gently wash the area with soap and running water. Do not soak the wound.
If the bite is on the hand or wrist, get in-person care today (urgent care or emergency department), even if it seems mild.

Get urgent care immediately if redness is spreading, swelling worsens, pus develops, pain increases, fever or chills occur, red streaks appear, numbness develops, or finger/hand movement becomes difficult.
Go to the emergency department now or call 911 if you feel very ill, develop confusion, or symptoms are rapidly worsening.
If you are not up to date on tetanus (or unsure), you should get a tetanus booster in person.
